items.title: Keynote
items.location: PreviousNext Visions Theatre, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
John Sheridan joined the APS Senior Executive Service in 2002, after 22 years in the Australian Army and three in the Defence Science and Technology Organisation. In Defence, he was the lead IT architect and, later, responsible for the design and development of IT systems.

 In the Australian Government Information Management Office, John negotiated the Microsoft Volume Sourcing Agreement, managed major ICT budget reductions, and led whole of government matters such as australia.gov.au, data centre strategy, coordinated procurement, industry engagement, and telecommunications services.

 In February 2013, John became the first Australian Government Chief Technology Officer. In addition to his responsibilities for ICT, coordinated and general procurement policy and operations, John assists the business community as the Australian Government Procurement Coordinator.
items.summary: John Sheridan joined the APS Senior Executive Service in 2002, after 22 years in the Australian Army and three in the Defence Science and Technology Organisation....

items.title: Open Source and Open Communities: enabling better government
items.location: PreviousNext Visions Theatre, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description: Pia will discuss how open source technologies, methodologies and ethos is helping governments improve.
items.summary: Pia will discuss how open source technologies, methodologies and ethos is helping governments improve....

items.title: Transforming a community engagement program into a modern web app
items.location: Oxide Interactive Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
This case study is about transforming a paper based behaviour change program into an interactive web application. The program is called 'Challenge to Change’ and it is currently run by the Maribyrnong City Council in Melbourne Victoria. The program takes place over a set time period with multiple data collection and data presentation steps and provides 2 different options depending on how engaged the user wants to be.

 The case study looks at the prototyping and design and development processes used to transform a 4 stage program and a lengthly work booklet into a online journey that will guide the user through the program on a web or mobile device. The app is built in Drupal which required some interesting configuration to enable a seamless step by step process for the user as they interact with the multiple forms, data visualisations and social media engagement points.

 A number of other Councils are interested in the app so it is being built in a way to make it possible to whitelabel it and to run similar programs but with different questions or data.
items.summary: This case study is about transforming a paper based behaviour change program into an interactive web application....

items.title: Web accessibility and Drupal
items.location: DATACOM Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
There are many accessibility rules and several sets of accessibility guildelines. Developing an accessible website is hard. Drupal can help you.

 The most important thing Drupal does for your website, is that by default it makes sure your website is not inaccessible. The road to accessibility is much longer though. We'll step through several processes you can put in place to ensure accessibility of a website.

 This session will introduce the different accessibility guildelines. What they are, which ones you should pay close attention to and how they apply to a website. We'll see which components of the accessbility rules relates to Drupal as a CMS, which parts relates to editors and webmasters and different tools you can use with Drupal to help you ensure a website stays accessible over time.
items.summary: There are many accessibility rules and several sets of accessibility guildelines....

items.title: Morning Tea
items.location: National Museum of Australia

items.title: Clinical Knowledge Network in Drupal - Two Week Build
items.location: Oxide Interactive Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Catalyst was contacted in June 2014 to support a state health authority with their Clinical Knowledge Network (CKN) requirements. This system references a wide range of clinical resources, for use by medical professionals in the course of their duties.

 A legacy CKN system was scheduled for urgent retirement and Drupal allowed us to go from nothing, to a built and deployed solution in a two week turnaround. This is why we all love Drupal.

 This two week body of work included:

 
 Site configuration and build

 Branding according to client style requirements

 Usability improvements

 Content migration

 Bespoke authentication module 

 Depoloyment into high availability AWS cloud hosting

 Using Drupal and the responsive and pragmatic client stakeholders allowed us to focus on rapid prototyping, giving instant feedback during the build phase.

 
 Continuous Integration was also a key component, allowing us to automate the pipeline releases. This included automated quality assurance processes and deployments. Allowing the team to focus on what really matters, the product, not the mundane technical processes.
items.summary: Catalyst was contacted in June 2014 to support a state health authority with their Clinical Knowledge Network (CKN) requirements....

items.title: Prioritising accessibility during the build of a site
items.location: DATACOM Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
This session focuses accessibility activities to perform at key stages of the web development lifecycle. These activities can ensure that a site is accessible as possible at launch.

 Activities discussed include:

- Request For Quote consultation

- Staff training

- Functional specification / Web Style Guide review

- Wireframes / Design evaluation

- Template evaluation

- Web site audit
items.summary: This session focuses accessibility activities to perform at key stages of the web development lifecycle....

items.title: The new Front-end work-flow: from ticketing to building
items.location: PreviousNext Visions Theatre, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
We've been doing it wrong. Our CSS sucks. Our ticketing structure is flawed. Our estimates are a joke. For over a decade, we've been styling websites using a top-left to bottom-right methodology with selectors chosen almost at random. Since we haven't been planning our entire front-end before building it, is it any wonder our ticketing system and estimates are so wrong?

 But there's no need to drown ourselves in beer. A new methodology called Design Components using techniques like OOCSS, SMACSS and BEM show us that planning before building can make our projects maintainable, easier to debug, and smaller with reduced CSS file sizes.

 And, surprise, surpise! Planning ahead means ticketing and estimation become much easier to deal with.

 This session will outline our current mistakes and then introduce the basic techniques for CSS layering and using design components, the heart of any front-end CSS project. We will also discuss ticket structure, project organization, and tricks to implement components when you can't change Drupal's classes.
items.summary: We've been doing it wrong....

items.title: Case Study: Manningham City Council
items.location: DATACOM Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Manningham City Council moved to a new Technocrat-Acquia D7 Multisite Platform in June 2014. The process was refreshingly agile. We were allowed to innovate.

 So let's talk about platform architecture, versatile content types, improved editorial workflows, Omega 4 sub-themeing, and Open Layers mapping.

 Join us for insights and discussion about this challenging and interesting project. 20 minute talk, extended QA.
items.summary: Manningham City Council moved to a new Technocrat-Acquia D7 Multisite Platform in June 2014....

items.title: Drupal 8 for Site Builders
items.location: Oxide Interactive Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Drupal 8 comes with a swag of improvements for Site Builders:

 
 Configuration management

 Fieldable blocks (and multiple instances of blocks)

 CKEditor

 New field types

 Display and form modes

 Reponsive image support

 Multingual improvements

 much much more...

 
 It's now easier than ever to build a Drupal site without 100's of contrib modules.

 Come to this session if you want to catch up on the latest improvements in Drupal 8 for site builders.
items.summary: Drupal 8 comes with a swag of improvements for Site Builders:...

items.location: PreviousNext Visions Theatre, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description: Cloud based services offer many advantages over running your own physical servers, so it's important to know of what's different so a site can be properly designed, implemented and maintained.

items.full_description:
Jeffrey A. "jam" McGuire, Open Source Evangelist at Acquia, has a longstanding passion for the Drupal project and its community and is involved at the intersection of open source software, business, and culture.

 A memorable and charismatic communicator, he receives enthusiastic responses from audiences at events around the world, where he talks about open source technology and community, digital disruption, Drupal and more.

 This helps satisfy his inner diva, which he also feeds with performances as a storyteller and musician.
items.summary: Jeffrey A....

items.title: aGov: Past, Present and Future
items.location: PreviousNext Visions Theatre, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
aGov is a Drupal distribution created for public sector websites in Australia by PreviousNext. Since its initial releases nearly 2 years ago, aGov has been downloaded over 11k times, and reports around 300 installations and growing.

 This session will look at:

- the history of aGov, why it was created and what needs it was trying to meet

- it's current state and development priorities

- the future of aGov, and where it might go in the next 2 to 3 years

 As part of this session, you will be able to ask questions about aGov and provide feedback or suggestions for future direction.
items.summary: aGov is a Drupal distribution created for public sector websites in Australia by PreviousNext....

items.title: Solving Drupal performance issues
items.location: Oxide Interactive Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
This session is for anyone that ever had a page on their site load slowly, outages due to performance issues, or a site that needed mountains of hardware to run at all.

 Here I will share a data based approach for identifying and solving performance issues, along with tips on the low hanging fruit we so often miss, common pitfalls and best practices.

 We often talk about Drupal Performance in relation to setting up Varnish, Boost, writing VCL files - I am not going to tell you any of this.

What we rarely talk about is what to do when performance issues are already there.

 Often we end up guessing what the issue might be or try to just "optimise all-the-things" which can be a never-ending (costly) task.

To solve performance issues we need to know what is actually happening, so we can choose to apply effort where it makes a difference.
items.summary: This session is for anyone that ever had a page on their site load slowly, outages due to performance issues, or a site that needed mountains of hardware to run at all....

items.title: The Drupal community: the real power of 8
items.location: DATACOM Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Drupal 8 is on the horizon with many powerful new features to look forward to. But when it's all boiled down, Drupal itself is much like other web content management technology. What truly sets Drupal apart is the phenomenal community behind its open source code base.

 Donna will share an insiders view of the community, give a sneak peek of what's coming in Drupal 8, and help you understand how to get the best out of Drupal by engaging with the community.
items.summary: Drupal 8 is on the horizon with many powerful new features to look forward to....

items.title: 2.9 million words in two months
items.location: DATACOM Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Most web teams face one inescapable truth - content will be late.

 This session is about how the Australian Pesticides and Veterinary Medicines Authority got on the front foot and reworked 2.9 million words in just two months.

 Review, rewrite, edit, approve and publish; without cutting any corners; and with only one person to coordinate.

 Learn how Drupal was used to efficiently solve this problem, and why email is the wrong tool for content development.
items.summary: Most web teams face one inescapable truth - content will be late....

items.title: Heartbleed: hardening the Acquia Cloud platform in double time
items.location: Oxide Interactive Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description: At Acquia, we care about security. The Heartbleed vulnerability kicked off our incident response plan which allowed us to respond in a swift and effective manner. In this case study I will walk you through what the Heartbleed vulnerabilty is, how were were able to harden thousands of Drupal sites against the vulnerability, what was involved and how long it took. I will also touch on some other security and performance incidents that we have faced and how we successfully responded to them.
items.summary: At Acquia, we care about security....

items.location: PreviousNext Visions Theatre, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description: An international view on how governments have been adopting open source technologies, including Drupal, to improve productivity, save money and engage the community.
items.summary: An international view on how governments have been adopting open source technologies, including Drupal, to improve productivity, save money and engage the community....

items.title: Breaking bad code
items.location: Oxide Interactive Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Testing is for monkeys! So automate it.

 Drupal sites can be complex. With that complexity comes the increased risk of changes inadvertently breaking things. Simple changes to CSS, a template, or a minor module update can have big implications for your website.

 So how do you ensure that your Drupal site remains functional long term, and incorporate changes with confidence?

 In this talk, Iris and Mikkolo will discuss automated testing techniques:

* Cross browser testing

* Functional testing

* Visual comparison testing

 We will demonstrate how to apply readily available tools such as Browserstack, Saucelabs, Selenium, PHPUnit, PhantomJS and a few others to automate this process, reduce risk and more effectively use developer time.
items.summary: Testing is for monkeys!...

items.title: Drupal and the future of the internet
items.location: PreviousNext Visions Theatre, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Drupal has cemented its place within the era of digital information, managing large volumes of content and publishing websites for audiences across the world.

 However as we move into the future, new trends are emerging off the back of ‘on demand’ cloud computing, mobile devices, crowdsourcing and apps. The focus will be less on getting content online, and more about how it’s delivered, analysed and personalised. This is critical for Government as it seeks to better provide information and services to the public.

 Dream with us as we talk about where the Internet is heading and how this fits with the future of Drupal.
items.summary: Drupal has cemented its place within the era of digital information, managing large volumes of content and publishing websites for audiences across the world....

items.title: Procedural to Object-Oriented Modules
items.location: DATACOM Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
A practical session on porting or writing modules to follow modern best practice object oriented PHP (assumes basic knowledge of object-oriented PHP). Key points are:

 
 Iterative refactoring procedural code into modern object oriented PHP

 Making Drupal 7 modules Drupal 8 ready

 Common Drupal 8 Design Patterns (Mediator, Dependency Injection, Factory)

 Important changes in Drupal 8 that affect module development (eg. hook_menu to routes)

 Automatic tools to assist in conversion of Drupal 7 modules to Drupal 8 (I am the author of Pharborist which is one such tool)

 Unit testing and mocking to drive higher quality code and faster development
items.summary: A practical session on porting or writing modules to follow modern best practice object oriented PHP (assumes basic knowledge of object-oriented PHP)....

items.title: Consultation forum as a service for the NZ Government
items.location: DATACOM Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Background

 Catalyst IT built a consultation forum as a service offering for NZ Government departments to use. It had requirements to be:

 * create a re-usable consultation forum

* reduce flexibility and offer more of a 'canned' site

*be the 'easy' option for NZ Government looking for consultations

* be hosted on aegir with minimum setup effort needed.

 Who this session is for:

Business managers, and Drupal developers looking to see how the initial build was done, how this functionality was abstracted into a re-usable product, and the features currently in plase and on the roadmap.

 This case study will show how the more tricky parts of the build were developed, and highlight modules that worked, as well as others that did not.

 Covered in the session:

* Features currently on offer in the platform

*How a forum with no login and SPAM is possible even without a captcha, this showcases a new comment scoring system soon to be opensourced

*Analytics and dashboarding of real time data

*How a team of forum moderators can work together

*The different type of consultation types on offer, and the types of feedback that can be gathered

*The impact this has around price compared to a traditional Drupal site build
items.summary: Background...

items.title: Facilitating Code Collaboration with Drupal in Government
items.location: PreviousNext Visions Theatre, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Given the rise of Drupal within Government, how are web teams adapting to Drupal's inherent openness as a platform for collaboration and sharing?

 This session will provide an overview of initiatives already in place within Australian Government agencies using Drupal, with ideas and approaches that can be adopted from around the Drupal world.

 Suitable for web team members, managers and executive-level decision makers, the talk will provide calls to action to help your organisation reduce duplicated effort whilst encouraging interaction with other agencies and becoming productive members of the Drupal communiuty.
items.summary: Given the rise of Drupal within Government, how are web teams adapting to Drupal's inherent openness as a platform for collaboration and sharing?...

items.title: Isolate your Drupal sites with Docker and Puppet
items.location: Oxide Interactive Room, National Museum of Australia
items.full_description:
Traditionally isolating projects would have been done with separate hosts which can be costly in time and money. With the perfect blend of Puppet and Docker this doesn't have to be.

 This talk covers both Puppet and Docker and there will be plenty of take away coding samples.

 Attendees will have the opportunity to ask as many questions as they want about any of these technologies and/or configuration. This is also a good opitunity to organise and split off into BOF's afterwards.

 The talk has already been performed at the Drupal Melbourne meetup, a link can be found here: http://www.previousnext.com.au/b... . The proposed talk will be refined from the linked Melbourne presentation.
items.summary: Traditionally isolating projects would have been done with separate hosts which can be costly in time and money....
